Quando um sistema precisa processar uma propriedade específica (e.g., posição X) de milhares de entidades, a CPU carrega cada objeto completo para cache, desperdiçando bandwidth com propriedades não utilizadas. No modelo SoA, cada propriedade reside em seu próprio array contíguo, permitindo que a CPU faça prefetch otimizado de dados relevantes.2.3 Entity Component System (ECS)O padrão Entity Component System representa a aplicação de DOD ao domínio de motores de jogos e simulações. Neste modelo: Entities são identificadores únicos (tipicamente inteiros); Components são dados puros sem comportamento, armazenados em arrays tipados; Systems são funções que processam conjuntos específicos de components em loops otimizados.A separação entre dados (components) e lógica (systems) permite otimizações impossíveis em OOD: memória pode ser pré-alocada em startup, systems podem ser paralelizados via Web Workers, e serialização torna-se trivial através de ArrayBuffer. Bibliotecas como bitECS demonstram ganhos de performance de até 50x comparado a alternativas baseadas em objetos como ECSY.3. Estudos de Caso3.1 Arquitetura do FigmaO Figma representa o estado da arte em ferramentas de design profissional executando integralmente no navegador. Sua arquitetura demonstra princípios fundamentais aplicáveis ao problema em estudo. O motor de renderização é implementado em C++ e compilado para WebAssembly, resultando em tempo de carregamento 3x menor comparado à implementação anterior em asm.js.Criticamente, o Figma não utiliza HTML/CSS para o canvas principal. Todo o documento é renderizado via WebGL com engine customizada. O gerenciamento de estado reside em estruturas de dados C++ no heap do WASM. A interface React interage com a engine via bridge estreita, recebendo snapshots ou subscrevendo a eventos específicos, mas nunca "possuindo" o estado do documento.O sistema de undo/redo emprega o Command Pattern com serialização binária de deltas, evitando completamente snapshots de objetos JavaScript. Esta abordagem mantém o histórico de operações em formato compacto, independente do tamanho do documento. O sistema multiplayer utiliza CRDTs em nível de propriedade, simplificando a resolução de conflitos através de semântica last-writer-wins arbitrada pelo servidor.3.2 Sistemas Geoespaciais: Google Maps e CesiumSistemas como Google Maps e CesiumJS gerenciam estado através de hierarquias de tiles com Level of Detail (LOD) dinâmico. O mundo é subdividido em tiles endereçáveis por coordenada e nível de zoom. No zoom 0, um único tile cobre o globo; no zoom 14, existem mais de 268 milhões de tiles.O gerenciamento de estado foca em ciclo de vida de tiles (unloaded → loading → loaded → cached → expired), queries de viewport via indexação espacial, e transições de LOD baseadas em Screen Space Error. CesiumJS implementa skip-level loading, que permite pular níveis intermediários de detalhe quando tiles de maior resolução podem ser carregados diretamente, resultando em redução de 30-50% no uso de memória.A biblioteca deck.gl, construída sobre WebGL, demonstra capacidade de renderização de até 1 milhão de entidades a 60fps, com degradação para 10-20fps em 10 milhões de entidades. Esta performance é alcançada através de batching agressivo de draw calls, instanced rendering, e uso de binary data formats para transmissão de dados.3.3 Motores de Jogos WebGLJogos WebGL pesados empregam uso estrito de TypedArrays, evitando new Object() no loop principal. A técnica de Object Pooling permite reutilização de instâncias, eliminando pressão sobre o GC. Atualizações de estado ocorrem em physics ticks fixos, desacoplados do render tick, permitindo simulação determinística independente de frame rate.Engines como Babylon.js e PlayCanvas implementam scene graphs otimizados com dirty flagging para minimizar recálculos de transformação. O estado do mundo é frequentemente particionado em chunks ou octrees para permitir culling eficiente e streaming sob demanda. Web Workers são utilizados para offload de física e pathfinding quando SharedArrayBuffer está disponível.4. Análise Comparativa de External StoresA seleção de uma biblioteca de gerenciamento de estado framework-agnostic requer análise de múltiplas dimensões: mecanismo de reatividade, overhead de bundle, ergonomia de API, e adequação a diferentes categorias de estado. A tabela a seguir sintetiza características das principais opções disponíveis.Tabela 1: Comparativo de bibliotecas de gerenciamento de estado framework-agnosticBibliotecaMecanismoBundleVanilla APIPerf. MobileAdequaçãoZustandPub/Sub imutável~1.2kbCompletaAltaEstado global leveValtioProxy-based~3kbCompletaMédia-AltaDX intuitivaLegend-StateSignal-based~4kbCompletaMuito AltaFine-grained UIMobXObservable TFRP~16kbCompletaAltaEstado derivadoNano StoresAtômico<1kbCompletaMuito AltaBundle mínimoPreact SignalsFine-grained~2kbCompletaExtremaBridge UI-EngineA análise revela trade-offs significativos entre as abordagens. Stores baseados em Proxy (Valtio, MobX) oferecem excelente experiência de desenvolvedor através de mutação direta e tracking automático de dependências, porém introduzem overhead de trap que se manifesta em loops de alta frequência. Stores imutáveis (Redux, Zustand) evitam este overhead mas requerem clonagem de objetos a cada update, gerando pressão sobre o GC em estruturas profundas ou frequentemente atualizadas.Signals representam um ponto ótimo para binding de UI, oferecendo updates O(1) ou O(n) onde n é apenas o número de listeners diretos, sem VDOM diffing. Entretanto, o overhead de objetos JavaScript por signal torna-os inadequados como estrutura primária para simulação física de larga escala. A conclusão é que nenhuma solução única atende a todos os requisitos; uma arquitetura híbrida é necessária.5. Estratégias de Implementação de Undo/Redo5.1 Command PatternO Command Pattern encapsula operações como objetos com métodos execute() e undo(), permitindo reverter ações através de execução inversa. Esta abordagem é ideal para operações com efeitos colaterais (e.g., chamadas de API) e permite composição de comandos complexos. A memória utilizada é proporcional ao número de operações no histórico, não ao tamanho do estado.A implementação requer definição de comandos para cada tipo de operação, com armazenamento dos dados necessários para reversão. Para uma operação de movimento de entidade, por exemplo, o comando armazena o ID da entidade, posição original e posição final. O CommandManager mantém stacks separadas para undo e redo, garantindo consistência semântica das operações.5.2 Patch-Based Undo com Structural SharingA alternativa baseada em patches utiliza bibliotecas como Immer para capturar deltas entre estados. Cada mutação gera patches (descrição das mudanças) e inverse patches (descrição para reverter). Esta abordagem oferece memória proporcional ao tamanho das mudanças, não do estado completo, através de structural sharing.O middleware zundo adiciona capacidade de undo/redo a stores Zustand em menos de 700 bytes. Configurações incluem limite de histórico, seleção parcial de estado a rastrear, e função de igualdade customizável. O padrão de pausar histórico durante operações de drag permite agrupar múltiplas micro-mudanças em um único passo de undo.Tabela 2: Comparativo de abordagens de undo/redoAbordagemMemóriaComplexidadeSide EffectsCaso de UsoFull SnapshotsAltaBaixaNão suportaPrototipação rápidaCommand PatternBaixaAltaEncapsulaApps complexasJSON PatchesMédiaMédiaNão suportaEstados grandesCRDT-basedMédiaMuito AltaColaborativoEdição multiplayer6. Restrições de Navegadores Móveis6.1 Limites de Memória por PlataformaNavegadores móveis impõem restrições de memória significativamente mais severas que ambientes desktop. iOS Safari limita memória total de página a 2-3GB incluindo GPU, com limite prático de canvas em 256MB e WebGL em aproximadamente 380MB. Chrome Android 64-bit permite até 4GB por tab, com variação significativa em dispositivos de entrada.Desenvolvimentos recentes indicam que WebGPU em iOS Safari oferece limites substancialmente maiores, com benchmarks em iPhone 16 Pro Max demonstrando aproximadamente 2.5GB disponíveis. Esta diferença representa oportunidade significativa para aplicações que podem adotar WebGPU, embora a tecnologia ainda esteja em estágio de adoção inicial.6.2 Mitigação de Pausas de GCObject Pooling é a técnica primária para eliminação de alocações em hot paths. Um pool mantém instâncias pré-criadas que são adquiridas, utilizadas e retornadas sem envolver o alocador de memória. Para dados numéricos, TypedArray-backed pools oferecem desempenho superior através de memória contígua e zero overhead de objeto JavaScript.Práticas adicionais incluem evitar closures em funções chamadas por frame (closures capturam escopo, gerando alocação), preferir loops for tradicionais sobre métodos de array (forEach cria novo contexto de função), e pré-computar valores constantes fora do loop de renderização. Profiling via Chrome DevTools Performance tab permite identificar alocações inesperadas.6.3 Touch Handling a 60fpsPassive event listeners são mandatórios para performance de scroll em dispositivos móveis. Quando um listener é registrado com { passive: true }, o navegador pode iniciar scroll imediatamente sem aguardar potencial chamada de preventDefault(). Para operações de desenho em canvas onde preventDefault() é necessário, listeners devem ser explicitamente marcados com { passive: false }.7. Arquitetura Proposta7.1 Modelo Dual-StoreA arquitetura recomendada implementa separação estrita entre Simulation State (estado do mundo/engine) e Application State (estado da interface). Esta divisão reflete a diferença fundamental nos requisitos de cada categoria: o estado de simulação opera em frequência de frame com milhares de entidades, enquanto o estado de aplicação responde a eventos discretos de usuário.Tabela 3: Características das camadas de estadoAspectoSimulation StateApplication StateTecnologiaTypedArrays / ECS (bitECS)Signals / ZustandArmazenamentoSharedArrayBuffer (Web Worker)Heap JavaScriptMutabilidadeDireta (performance)Imutável (rastreabilidade)AcessoÍndices / PonteirosSelectors / SubscriptionsReatividadeNenhuma (polling)Fine-grained automáticaFrequência60fps (contínua)30fps ou event-driven7.2 Diagrama de CamadasA arquitetura completa organiza-se em seis camadas com responsabilidades distintas e interfaces bem definidas:Camada 1 - React UI Layer: Componentes React para toolbars, painéis e diálogos. Conecta-se a stores externos via useSyncExternalStore. Não possui estado de simulação; recebe apenas dados necessários para exibição.Camada 2 - External State Stores: Zustand para estado global de UI (tool selecionada, painéis abertos). Legend-State ou Signals para binding fino de dados de entidade selecionada. Command Manager para gerenciamento de histórico undo/redo.Camada 3 - Core Data Layer: R-tree (RBush) para indexação espacial e queries de viewport. TypedArray Entity Store para dados de posição, escala, rotação. Object Pools para vetores temporários e resultados de cálculo.Camada 4 - Sync Loop: Scheduler centralizado executando em ordem fixa: updatePhysics() → renderCanvas() → syncUI(). A sincronização de UI opera em frequência reduzida (30fps) para evitar overhead de React.Camada 5 - Render Layer: Canvas 2D ou WebGL operando via requestAnimationFrame. Lê diretamente de TypedArrays. Viewport culling via queries em R-tree. Batching de draw calls para minimizar state changes de GPU.Camada 6 - Worker Layer (opcional): OffscreenCanvas para renderização em thread separada. SharedArrayBuffer para compartilhamento zero-copy de dados entre main thread e workers. Processamento de física e pathfinding em background.8. Recomendações de ImplementaçãoCom base na análise conduzida, as seguintes recomendações são propostas para implementação de um editor geoespacial de alta performance:Estado de Entidades: Implementar store customizado baseado em TypedArrays com padrão Structure of Arrays. Para 100.000 entidades com posição (x, y) e status, a memória base é aproximadamente 800KB (2 × Float32Array × 100.000 + Uint8Array × 100.000), versus dezenas de MB para objetos JavaScript equivalentes.Indexação Espacial: Utilizar RBush para R-tree. Bulk loading de 1 milhão de itens completa em aproximadamente 1600ms; queries de viewport com 1000 resultados completam em aproximadamente 4ms. Reconstruir índice apenas quando entidades são criadas/deletadas, não em cada movimento.Estado de UI: Zustand vanilla API para estado global (ferramenta ativa, modo de edição). Simplicidade de API, bundle mínimo (~1.2kb), e DevTools excelentes. Evitar imutabilidade profunda; preferir estruturas flat.Bridge UI-Engine: @preact/signals-core para binding de dados de entidade selecionada a componentes React. Signals permitem updates de DOM sem re-render de componente. Throttle de sincronização a 30fps ou menos.Undo/Redo: Command Pattern para operações estruturais (criar, deletar, agrupar). Patches (zundo) para mudanças de propriedade. Limite de histórico em 100-200 entradas. Pausar histórico durante drag operations.Renderização: Canvas 2D para MVP; migrar para WebGL/WebGPU para escala. requestAnimationFrame como driver de loop. Nunca acessar DOM no loop de render. Viewport culling via R-tree antes de cada frame.Mobile: Object pooling para todos vetores temporários. Passive touch listeners como padrão. Monitorar memória via performance.memory (Chrome) ou estimativa de ArrayBuffer sizes. LOD agressivo para tiles fora do centro de viewport.9. Análise de RiscosTabela 4: Matriz de riscos e mitigaçõesRiscoProb.ImpactoMitigaçãoLimites de memória iOSAltaCríticoLOD agressivo, unload de tiles, monitoramento de memóriaGC pauses em animaçãoMédiaAltoObject pooling, TypedArrays, evitar closures em hot pathsReact render thrashingMédiaMédioExternal stores, throttled UI updates (30fps)Crescimento undo stackMédiaMédioLimite de histórico, compressão de patchesLatência touch eventsBaixaAltoPassive listeners, estado de gesto separado de ReactDessincronização UI/CanvasMédiaBaixoScheduler centralizado, ordem fixa de execuçãoErgonomia TypedArrayAltaBaixoViews/Cursors para DX em código não-crítico10. ConclusõesA investigação conduzida demonstra que alcançar performance de nível Figma em navegadores móveis requer mudança arquitetural fundamental, não otimização incremental de padrões existentes. Nenhuma quantidade de React.memo ou useMemo compensará a incompatibilidade fundamental entre reconciliação de Virtual DOM e orçamentos de frame de 16ms.Os principais insights derivados desta pesquisa são: (1) A localização do estado é determinante; estado de alta frequência deve residir fora de frameworks reativos em TypedArrays com acesso direto. (2) O layout de dados importa; Structure of Arrays oferece ganhos de ordem de magnitude para iteração de estado numérico. (3) Gerenciamento de memória é crítico em mobile; object pooling e pré-alocação eliminam pausas de GC. (4) Estruturas espaciais são essenciais em escala; R-trees e quadtrees habilitam queries eficientes para 100k+ entidades. (5) Complexidade de undo escala com a aplicação; iniciar com patches, graduar para commands conforme necessário.A arquitetura recomendada para um editor geoespacial similar ao Google Maps com suporte a undo/redo compreende: Zustand para estado de UI, Legend-State ou store customizado para entidades, RBush para indexação espacial, Command Pattern com histórico baseado em patches para undo/redo, e renderização Canvas/WebGL completamente externa a React. Esta combinação foi validada em aplicações de produção desde Figma até tldraw até CesiumJS.Trabalhos futuros incluem benchmarking quantitativo das configurações propostas em dispositivos móveis representativos, investigação de WebGPU como alternativa de maior performance a WebGL, e desenvolvimento de abstrações que melhorem ergonomia de desenvolvimento sem sacrificar performance.ReferênciasFigma Engineering. "Building a professional design tool on the web." Figma Blog, 2017.Figma Engineering. "WebAssembly cut Figma's load time by 3x." Figma Blog, 2017.Wallace, E. "How Figma's multiplayer technology works." madebyevan.com, 2019.Cesium. "Skipping Levels of Detail." Cesium Blog, 2017.deck.gl. "Performance Optimization." deck.gl Documentation.pmndrs. "Zustand: Bear necessities for state management in React." GitHub Repository.LegendApp. "Legend-State: Super fast and powerful state library." GitHub Repository.charkour. "Zundo: Undo/redo middleware for Zustand." GitHub Repository.Wonderland Engine. "WebGL Performance on Safari and Apple Vision Pro." Wonderland Engine Blog, 2024.Airtight Interactive. "Building a 60FPS WebGL Game on Mobile." Airtight Interactive Blog, 2015.RBush. "A high-performance JavaScript R-tree library." GitHub Repository.bitECS. "Functional, minimal, data-driven ECS library." GitHub Repository.

content.thinking:
Excelente! Consegui extrair o conteúdo do documento. Este é um relatório técnico-científico muito completo sobre arquiteturas de gerenciamento de estado para aplicações web complexas. O documento aborda:

1. **Problema do GC (Garbage Collection)**: O GC é o gargalo principal, não a renderização
2. **Data-Oriented Design (DOD)**: Structure of Arrays (SoA) vs Array of Structures (AoS)
3. **Entity Component System (ECS)**: Padrão para motores de jogos
4. **Estudos de caso**: Figma, Google Maps, CesiumJS, jogos WebGL
5. **Bibliotecas comparadas**: Zustand, Valtio, Legend-State, MobX, Nano Stores, Preact Signals
6. **Estratégias de Undo/Redo**: Command Pattern vs Patch-based
7. **Restrições de mobile**: Object pooling, TypedArrays, passive listeners
8. **Arquitetura Dual-Store proposta**

Agora preciso criar ou identificar a task relevante em features.json para o planejamento de refatoração do state management. Vou criar uma nova task de arquitetura/tech-debt que planeje a implementação dessas recomendações.

Olhando o features.json, vejo que TECH-DEBT-001 já foi completada e migrou o camera state para @mms/toolkit. Mas as recomendações do documento vão muito além disso - elas propõem uma arquitetura completamente diferente baseada em:

1. TypedArrays para estado de simulação
2. Dual-Store (Simulation State separado de Application State)
3. Entity Component System (ECS) para as entidades/tasks
4. R-tree (RBush) para indexação espacial


5. Object Pooling
6. Signals para UI binding

Identifico duas estratégias cruciais para otimização de performance: object pooling para gerenciamento eficiente de memória e signals para binding reativo de interface. Essas técnicas podem reduzir significativamente a sobrecarga de garbage collection e melhorar a responsividade da aplicação.

Agora preciso criar a task STATE-ARCH-001 no features.json. Vou criar uma task de alta prioridade que capture todas as recomendações do documento de pesquisa.

As principais recomendações do documento são:

1. **Arquitetura Dual-Store**:
   - Simulation State: TypedArrays/ECS para estado de alta frequência (60fps)
   - Application State: Signals/Zustand para UI

2. **Para o estado de entidades (tasks)**:
   - TypedArrays com padrão Structure of Arrays (SoA)
   - bitECS ou similar para ECS
   - ~800KB para 100k entidades vs dezenas de MB com objetos JS

3. **Indexação Espacial**:
   - RBush para R-tree
   - Queries de viewport eficientes

4. **Estado de UI**:
   - Zustand vanilla API (já temos!)
   - Evitar imutabilidade profunda, preferir estruturas flat

5. **Bridge UI-Engine**:
   - @preact/signals-core para binding de entidade selecionada
   - Throttle de sincronização a 30fps

6. **Undo/Redo**:
   - Command Pattern para operações estruturais
   - Patches (zundo) para mudanças de propriedade

7. **Mobile Optimizations**:
   - Object pooling
   - Passive touch listeners
   - LOD agressivo

Vou criar a task no features.json com todos esses detalhes.

Implementação deve ser incremental, iniciando com EntityStore e spatial index, depois bridge de UI e undo/redo. 2025-12-17."

content.thinking: Agora tenho visão da estrutura do toolkit. Vou ler alguns arquivos chave para entender a API e atualizar a task STATE-ARCH-001 para usar @mms/toolkit em vez de Zustand.

O @mms/toolkit tem uma implementação completa de state machine. Agora vou atualizar a task STATE-ARCH-001 no features.json para substituir todas as referências ao Zustand pelo @mms/toolkit.

A arquitetura do toolkit já tem:
- createMachine - para criar máquinas de estado
- Slices - para modularizar o estado
- Immer - para imutabilidade
- useMachine / useSelector - hooks para React
- dispatch / getState - API similar ao Redux/Zustand
- Cache policies - para invalidação de cache

Vou atualizar a task para usar @mms/toolkit para UI state.

content.text: O @mms/toolkit já tem uma implementação robusta de state machine com Immer. Vou atualizar a task STATE-ARCH-001 para usar o toolkit em vez do Zustand.

{
  "project": "Metro Integrated Planning System (MMS)",
  "version": "0.3.1",
  "lastUpdated": "2025-12-17",
  "product": {
    "entry": "apps/canvas",
    "description": "Production React app with semantic zoom (Vite + React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS 6)"
  },
  "reference": {
    "entry": "design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html",
    "description": "Standalone POC for visual reference only"
  },
  "priorityQueue": {
    "description": "Features ordered by execution priority. Execute in order, respecting blockedBy dependencies.",
    "note": "Priority 0 = immediate, Priority 1 = high, Priority 2 = after dependencies"
  },
  "parallelExecution": {
    "description": "Parallel execution configuration for remaining features (updated 2025-12-17)",
    "runner": "bun scripts/parallel-runner.ts",
    "worktreeDir": ".trees/",
    "status": "MOSTLY COMPLETE - Only 6 features remain",
    "waves": [
      {
        "wave": 1,
        "description": "Navigation enhancements + Task panel (can run in parallel)",
        "maxConcurrency": 3,
        "groups": [
          {"id": "group-A", "features": ["F016"], "sequential": false, "files": ["InfiniteCanvas.tsx"], "note": "Shift+Scroll vertical pan"},
          {"id": "group-B", "features": ["F026"], "sequential": false, "files": ["App.tsx"], "note": "Task details panel"},
          {"id": "group-C", "features": ["F027", "F028"], "sequential": true, "files": ["InfiniteCanvas.tsx"], "note": "Keyboard shortcuts zoom/pan"}
        ]
      },
      {
        "wave": 2,
        "description": "Accessibility (depends on Wave 1)",
        "maxConcurrency": 1,
        "dependsOn": 1,
        "groups": [
          {"id": "group-D", "features": ["F021"], "sequential": false, "files": ["InfiniteCanvas.tsx", "layers"], "note": "Keyboard task navigation"}
        ]
      },
      {
        "wave": 3,
        "description": "Final accessibility (depends on F021)",
        "maxConcurrency": 1,
        "dependsOn": 2,
        "groups": [
          {"id": "group-E", "features": ["F022"], "sequential": false, "files": ["InfiniteCanvas.tsx", "MicroLayer.tsx"], "note": "ARIA labels"}
        ]
      }
    ]
  },
  "features": [
    {
      "id": "STATE-ARCH-001",
      "category": "architecture",
      "priority": 1,
      "description": "Refatorar arquitetura de state management para padrão Dual-Store de alta performance",
      "passes": false,
      "blockedBy": null,
      "research": "docs/state-management-research/relatorio-state-management.pdf",
      "rationale": "Documento de pesquisa identifica GC como gargalo principal em aplicações WebGL móveis. React Hooks e stores reativos são inadequados para updates a 60fps. Propõe arquitetura Dual-Store separando Simulation State (TypedArrays/ECS) de Application State (Signals/Zustand).",
      "implementationPlan": {
        "phase1_foundation": {
          "description": "Criar infraestrutura base para Entity Store",
          "tasks": [
            "Criar EntityStore baseado em TypedArrays com padrão Structure of Arrays (SoA)",
            "Implementar Float32Array para posições (startDay, duration) e Uint8Array para flags (hasConflict, type)",
            "Criar Views/Cursors para acesso ergonômico aos dados tipados",
            "Benchmark: 100k entidades devem ocupar ~800KB vs dezenas de MB com objetos JS"
          ]
        },
        "phase2_spatial_index": {
          "description": "Implementar indexação espacial com R-tree",
          "tasks": [
            "Instalar RBush para R-tree (npm i rbush)",
            "Criar wrapper para inserção/query de tasks por viewport bounds",
            "Otimizar: bulk loading em startup, rebuild apenas em create/delete",
            "Target: queries de viewport com 1000 resultados em <5ms"
          ]
        },
        "phase3_ui_bridge": {
          "description": "Criar bridge entre Engine State e UI via Signals",
          "tasks": [
            "Instalar @preact/signals-core (~2kb)",
            "Criar selectedEntity signal para binding de TaskDetailsPanel",
            "Throttle de sincronização Engine→UI a 30fps máximo",
            "Manter Zustand existente para estado global de UI (tools, panels)"
          ]
        },
        "phase4_object_pooling": {
          "description": "Implementar Object Pooling para vetores temporários",
          "tasks": [
            "Criar VectorPool para Vector3/Vector2 temporários no render loop",
            "Criar ResultPool para arrays de resultados de queries",
            "Eliminar 'new Object()' e closures no hot path (requestAnimationFrame)",
            "Validar via Chrome DevTools Memory tab - zero GC spikes durante pan/zoom"
          ]
        },
        "phase5_undo_redo": {
          "description": "Implementar sistema de Undo/Redo",
          "tasks": [
            "Implementar Command Pattern para operações estruturais (create/delete/group)",
            "Usar zundo/patches para mudanças de propriedade (move, resize)",
            "Limite de histórico: 100-200 entradas",
            "Pausar histórico durante drag operations (agrupar micro-mudanças)"
          ]
        }
      },
      "performanceTargets": {
        "entities": "100.000+ tasks renderizáveis",
        "fps": "60fps constante durante pan/zoom",
        "memory": "<100MB para 100k entidades",
        "gcPauses": "Zero pausas perceptíveis durante interação",
        "queryTime": "<5ms para viewport queries"
      },
      "libraries": {
        "entityStore": "Custom TypedArray-based (Structure of Arrays pattern)",
        "spatialIndex": "RBush (R-tree)",
        "uiState": "Zustand (já implementado)",
        "uiBridge": "@preact/signals-core",
        "undoRedo": "Command Pattern + zundo",
        "ecs": "bitECS (opcional, se complexidade justificar)"
      },
      "notes": "PENDING - Task criada baseada em docs/state-management-research/relatorio-state-management.docx. Documento analisa arquiteturas de Figma, Google Maps, CesiumJS e motores de jogos WebGL. Recomenda Dual-Store: Simulation State (TypedArrays, 60fps) separado de Application State (Signals, event-driven). Implementação deve ser incremental, iniciando com EntityStore e spatial index, depois bridge de UI e undo/redo. 2025-12-17."
    },
    {
      "id": "URGENT-001",
      "category": "harness",
      "priority": 2,
      "description": "Auditar e documentar uso de React hooks - migrar apenas estados críticos para external stores",
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": null,
      "notes": "COMPLETE (REAVALIADO) - Audit done: cameraPosRef workaround resolves critical stale closure bug (F122). Other states (isInitialized, canvasSize, isPanning, panStart) are UI-only or one-time - no issues. useRef for momentum/animation is correct pattern. Added React hooks vs external stores guidelines to CLAUDE.md. Full migration to external stores would be over-engineering. If new stale closure bugs appear, migrate case by case. 2025-12-16."
    },
    {
      "id": "PERF-001",
      "category": "performance",
      "priority": 0,
      "description": "Adicionar ferramenta de desenvolvimento para métricas de performance WebGL em tempo real + logging detalhado",
      "passes": true,

  "categories": {
    "harness": "Harness infrastructure and testing protocol improvements",

    "core": "Essential functionality for the canvas to work",
    "navigation": "Pan, zoom, and movement interactions",
    "lod": "Level of Detail rendering (Macro/Meso/Micro)",
    "ui": "User interface elements (sidebar, header, panels)",
    "visual": "Visual design, colors, and styling",
    "interaction": "Click, hover, and selection behaviors",
    "accessibility": "Keyboard navigation and screen reader support",
    "performance": "Speed and optimization",
    "ux": "User experience enhancements",
    "canvas": "Production React app (Vite + React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS)"
  },
  "testingProtocol": {
    "description": "Mandatory testing sequence for each feature (see docs/QA_PROTOCOL.md)",
    "sequence": [
      "1. TYPECHECK: Run bun run typecheck",
      "2. VISUAL TEST: Use Playwright MCP to verify UI renders correctly",
      "3. CONSOLE CHECK: Verify no errors in browser console",
      "4. INTERACTION TEST: Test pan/zoom/LOD transitions if applicable",
      "5. PERFORMANCE CHECK (MANDATORY): Press 'P' to show perf panel, verify FPS >= 60 idle. For interaction changes, use __PERF_START_SESSION__/__PERF_STOP_SESSION__ for detailed analysis",
      "6. MARK COMPLETE: Only set passes:true after all tests pass"
    ],
    "performanceCriteria": {
      "idle": {
        "fpsAvg": ">=60",
        "fpsP1": ">=50",
        "framesBelow30": 0
      },
      "interaction": {
        "fpsAvg": ">=30",
        "fpsP1": ">=20",
        "framesBelow30": "<10"
      }
    },
    "note": "Full protocol documented in docs/QA_PROTOCOL.md. Performance testing is MANDATORY since HARNESS-003."
  },
  "completionCriteria": "Project is complete when ALL features with priority <= 4 have passes:true",
  "archive": {
    "description": "Features that are no longer relevant or have been superseded",
    "archivedFeatures": [
      {
        "id": "F117",
        "description": "Full visual parity with POC reference",
        "status": "archived",
        "notes": "No longer relevant - production app has surpassed POC in functionality and UX. POC served its purpose as initial validation."
      },
      {
        "id": "F024-POC",
        "description": "Canvas maintains 60fps",
        "status": "archived",
        "notes": "Covered by F116 (Performance optimization) in production. Archived per HARNESS-004."
      }
    ],
    "migrationNote": "HARNESS-004 (2025-12-16): Migrated F016-F023, F025-F028 from archive to active features list. Removed -POC suffix and assigned proper priorities."
  }
}

{
  "project": "Metro Integrated Planning System (MMS)",
  "version": "0.3.1",
  "lastUpdated": "2025-12-17",
  "product": {
    "entry": "apps/canvas",
    "description": "Production React app with semantic zoom (Vite + React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS 6)"
  },
  "reference": {
    "entry": "design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html",
    "description": "Standalone POC for visual reference only"
  },
  "priorityQueue": {
    "description": "Features ordered by execution priority. Execute in order, respecting blockedBy dependencies.",
    "note": "Priority 0 = immediate, Priority 1 = high, Priority 2 = after dependencies"
  },
  "parallelExecution": {
    "description": "Parallel execution configuration for remaining features (updated 2025-12-17)",
    "runner": "bun scripts/parallel-runner.ts",
    "worktreeDir": ".trees/",
    "status": "MOSTLY COMPLETE - Only 6 features remain",
    "waves": [
      {
        "wave": 1,
        "description": "Navigation enhancements + Task panel (can run in parallel)",
        "maxConcurrency": 3,
        "groups": [
          {"id": "group-A", "features": ["F016"], "sequential": false, "files": ["InfiniteCanvas.tsx"], "note": "Shift+Scroll vertical pan"},
          {"id": "group-B", "features": ["F026"], "sequential": false, "files": ["App.tsx"], "note": "Task details panel"},
          {"id": "group-C", "features": ["F027", "F028"], "sequential": true, "files": ["InfiniteCanvas.tsx"], "note": "Keyboard shortcuts zoom/pan"}
        ]
      },
      {
        "wave": 2,
        "description": "Accessibility (depends on Wave 1)",
        "maxConcurrency": 1,
        "dependsOn": 1,
        "groups": [
          {"id": "group-D", "features": ["F021"], "sequential": false, "files": ["InfiniteCanvas.tsx", "layers"], "note": "Keyboard task navigation"}
        ]
      },
      {
        "wave": 3,
        "description": "Final accessibility (depends on F021)",
        "maxConcurrency": 1,
        "dependsOn": 2,
        "groups": [
          {"id": "group-E", "features": ["F022"], "sequential": false, "files": ["InfiniteCanvas.tsx", "MicroLayer.tsx"], "note": "ARIA labels"}
        ]
      }
    ]
  },
  "features": [
    {
      "id": "STATE-ARCH-001",
      "category": "architecture",
      "priority": 1,
      "description": "Refatorar arquitetura de state management para padrão Dual-Store de alta performance",
      "passes": false,
      "blockedBy": null,
      "research": "docs/state-management-research/relatorio-state-management.pdf",
      "rationale": "Documento de pesquisa identifica GC como gargalo principal em aplicações WebGL móveis. React Hooks e stores reativos são inadequados para updates a 60fps. Propõe arquitetura Dual-Store separando Simulation State (TypedArrays/ECS) de Application State (@mms/toolkit state-machine). NOTA: Usamos @mms/toolkit (packages/toolkit) para UI state, NUNCA Zustand.",
      "implementationPlan": {
        "phase1_foundation": {
          "description": "Criar infraestrutura base para Entity Store",
          "tasks": [
            "Criar EntityStore baseado em TypedArrays com padrão Structure of Arrays (SoA)",
            "Implementar Float32Array para posições (startDay, duration) e Uint8Array para flags (hasConflict, type)",
            "Criar Views/Cursors para acesso ergonômico aos dados tipados",
            "Benchmark: 100k entidades devem ocupar ~800KB vs dezenas de MB com objetos JS"
          ]
        },
        "phase2_spatial_index": {
          "description": "Implementar indexação espacial com R-tree",
          "tasks": [
            "Instalar RBush para R-tree (npm i rbush)",
            "Criar wrapper para inserção/query de tasks por viewport bounds",
            "Otimizar: bulk loading em startup, rebuild apenas em create/delete",
            "Target: queries de viewport com 1000 resultados em <5ms"
          ]
        },
        "phase3_ui_bridge": {
          "description": "Criar bridge entre Engine State e UI via Signals",
          "tasks": [
            "Instalar @preact/signals-core (~2kb)",
            "Criar selectedEntity signal para binding de TaskDetailsPanel",
            "Throttle de sincronização Engine→UI a 30fps máximo",
            "Manter Zustand existente para estado global de UI (tools, panels)"
          ]
        },
        "phase4_object_pooling": {
          "description": "Implementar Object Pooling para vetores temporários",
          "tasks": [
            "Criar VectorPool para Vector3/Vector2 temporários no render loop",
            "Criar ResultPool para arrays de resultados de queries",
            "Eliminar 'new Object()' e closures no hot path (requestAnimationFrame)",
            "Validar via Chrome DevTools Memory tab - zero GC spikes durante pan/zoom"
          ]
        },
        "phase5_undo_redo": {
          "description": "Implementar sistema de Undo/Redo",
          "tasks": [
            "Implementar Command Pattern para operações estruturais (create/delete/group)",
            "Usar zundo/patches para mudanças de propriedade (move, resize)",
            "Limite de histórico: 100-200 entradas",
            "Pausar histórico durante drag operations (agrupar micro-mudanças)"
          ]
        }
      },
      "performanceTargets": {
        "entities": "100.000+ tasks renderizáveis",
        "fps": "60fps constante durante pan/zoom",
        "memory": "<100MB para 100k entidades",
        "gcPauses": "Zero pausas perceptíveis durante interação",
        "queryTime": "<5ms para viewport queries"
      },
      "libraries": {
        "entityStore": "Custom TypedArray-based (Structure of Arrays pattern)",
        "spatialIndex": "RBush (R-tree)",
        "uiState": "Zustand (já implementado)",
        "uiBridge": "@preact/signals-core",
        "undoRedo": "Command Pattern + zundo",
        "ecs": "bitECS (opcional, se complexidade justificar)"
      },
      "notes": "PENDING - Task criada baseada em docs/state-management-research/relatorio-state-management.docx. Documento analisa arquiteturas de Figma, Google Maps, CesiumJS e motores de jogos WebGL. Recomenda Dual-Store: Simulation State (TypedArrays, 60fps) separado de Application State (Signals, event-driven). Implementação deve ser incremental, iniciando com EntityStore e spatial index, depois bridge de UI e undo/redo. 2025-12-17."
    },
    {
      "id": "URGENT-001",
      "category": "harness",
      "priority": 2,
      "description": "Auditar e documentar uso de React hooks - migrar apenas estados críticos para external stores",
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": null,
      "notes": "COMPLETE (REAVALIADO) - Audit done: cameraPosRef workaround resolves critical stale closure bug (F122). Other states (isInitialized, canvasSize, isPanning, panStart) are UI-only or one-time - no issues. useRef for momentum/animation is correct pattern. Added React hooks vs external stores guidelines to CLAUDE.md. Full migration to external stores would be over-engineering. If new stale closure bugs appear, migrate case by case. 2025-12-16."
    },
    {
      "id": "PERF-001",
      "category": "performance",
      "priority": 0,
      "description": "Adicionar ferramenta de desenvolvimento para métricas de performance WebGL em tempo real + logging detalhado",
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": null,
      "notes": "COMPLETE - Implemented r3f-perf 7.2.3 with PerfMonitor component. Features: visual panel (FPS/MS/MB/GPU/draw calls/triangles), logging system with circular buffer (60s), session recording API (START_SESSION/STOP_SESSION). Toggle via 'P' key or navbar button. Global API: window.__PERF_LOG__, __PERF_EXPORT__, __PERF_START_SESSION__, __PERF_STOP_SESSION__, __PERF_CLEAR__. Known issue: r3f-perf has expandShaderIncludes console spam due to troika-three-utils compatibility with Three.js 0.170+ (tracked in r3f-perf#58). Does not affect functionality. Documented in docs/PERFORMANCE_LOGGING.md. Tested via Playwright MCP 2025-12-16."
    },
    {
      "id": "HARNESS-003",
      "category": "harness",
      "priority": 1,
      "description": "Atualizar protocolo de testes visuais do Harness para incluir métricas de performance (visual + logging)",
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "PERF-001",
      "notes": "COMPLETE - Updated docs/QA_PROTOCOL.md with comprehensive performance testing section. Added: Quick Check (visual) and Detailed Check (logging) approaches, Playwright integration examples, approval criteria matrix, when to use each approach guide, known issues section. Updated testingProtocol in features.json with performance criteria and mandatory step 5. Performance testing is now MANDATORY for all features. 2025-12-16."
    },
    {
      "id": "HARNESS-004",
      "category": "harness",
      "priority": 1,
      "description": "Migrar tarefas POC do archive para fluxo normal de features pendentes",
      "passes": true,

Removed -POC suffix, assigned priorities (2-4 based on importance), set proper blockedBy dependencies. F024-POC archived (covered by F116). Archive now only contains F117 and F024-POC. Added migrationNote to archive section. 2025-12-16."

    {

    {
      "id": "QA-PLAN-001",
      "category": "harness",
      "priority": 0,
      "description": "Plan QA testing protocol with deep thinking (planning mode)",
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "HARNESS-001",
      "notes": "COMPLETE - Created docs/QA_PROTOCOL.md with WebGL testing protocol. Updated session-start.sh and end-of-turn-check.sh hooks. Protocol includes: typecheck, visual test, console check, interaction test, performance check sequence."

Includes implementation recommendations for F118-F121."

Fix: Added cameraPosRef for synchronous position tracking. handlePointerDown now uses ref instead of state. All setCameraPos calls sync to ref via updateCameraPos(). Tested with rapid drag interruptions via Playwright MCP 2025-12-16."

      "implementationPlan": {

          "description": "Criar infraestrutura base para Entity Store",
          "tasks": [

            "Implementar Float32Array para posições (startDay, duration) e Uint8Array para flags (hasConflict, type)",
            "Criar Views/Cursors para acesso ergonômico aos dados tipados",
            "Benchmark: 100k entidades devem ocupar ~800KB vs dezenas de MB com objetos JS"
          ]
        },
        "phase2_spatial_index": {
          "description": "Implementar indexação espacial com R-tree",
          "tasks": [
            "Instalar RBush para R-tree (npm i rbush)",
            "Criar wrapper para inserção/query de tasks por viewport bounds",
            "Otimizar: bulk loading em startup, rebuild apenas em create/delete",
            "Target: queries de viewport com 1000 resultados em <5ms"
          ]
        },
        "phase3_ui_bridge": {
          "description": "Criar bridge entre Engine State e UI via @mms/toolkit",
          "tasks": [
            "Usar @mms/toolkit state-machine existente (packages/toolkit) para estado de UI",
            "Criar slice 'entities' com selectedEntityId, hoveredEntityId para binding",
            "useSyncExternalStore já implementado no toolkit - updates síncronos garantidos",
            "Throttle de sincronização Engine→UI a 30fps via scheduler (não pelo store)",
            "NUNCA usar Zustand - @mms/toolkit é a solução oficial do projeto"
          ]
        },
        "phase4_object_pooling": {
          "description": "Implementar Object Pooling para vetores temporários",
          "tasks": [
            "Criar VectorPool para Vector3/Vector2 temporários no render loop",
            "Criar ResultPool para arrays de resultados de queries",
            "Eliminar 'new Object()' e closures no hot path (requestAnimationFrame)",
            "Validar via Chrome DevTools Memory tab - zero GC spikes durante pan/zoom"
          ]
        },
        "phase5_undo_redo": {
          "description": "Implementar sistema de Undo/Redo",
          "tasks": [
            "Implementar Command Pattern para operações estruturais (create/delete/group)",
            "Usar zundo/patches para mudanças de propriedade (move, resize)",

            "Pausar histórico durante drag operations (agrupar micro-mudanças)"
          ]
        }

  "categories": {
    "harness": "Harness infrastructure and testing protocol improvements",

    "interaction": "Click, hover, and selection behaviors",

    "performance": "Speed and optimization",

  "testingProtocol": {
    "description": "Mandatory testing sequence for each feature (see docs/QA_PROTOCOL.md)",
    "sequence": [
      "1. TYPECHECK: Run bun run typecheck",
      "2. VISUAL TEST: Use Playwright MCP to verify UI renders correctly",
      "3. CONSOLE CHECK: Verify no errors in browser console",
      "4. INTERACTION TEST: Test pan/zoom/LOD transitions if applicable",
      "5. PERFORMANCE CHECK (MANDATORY): Press 'P' to show perf panel, verify FPS >= 60 idle. For interaction changes, use __PERF_START_SESSION__/__PERF_STOP_SESSION__ for detailed analysis",
      "6. MARK COMPLETE: Only set passes:true after all tests pass"
    ],
    "performanceCriteria": {
      "idle": {
        "fpsAvg": ">=60",
        "fpsP1": ">=50",
        "framesBelow30": 0
      },
      "interaction": {
        "fpsAvg": ">=30",
        "fpsP1": ">=20",
        "framesBelow30": "<10"
      }
    },
    "note": "Full protocol documented in docs/QA_PROTOCOL.md. Performance testing is MANDATORY since HARNESS-003."
  },
  "completionCriteria": "Project is complete when ALL features with priority <= 4 have passes:true",

270-### Key Rule
271-
272-**Do NOT write throwaway code without clear time/agility gains.** If code will be rewritten anyway, the simple version must provide faster iteration or validation—otherwise, just build it right the first time.
273-**If you know something will cause problems later (performance, UX, maintainability), address it now based on knowledge and testing—not fear, not speculation.**
274-

277-## Documentation Rules
278-
279-- **Language:** Always write documentation in English
280-- **Location:** Prefer updating existing docs over creating new files
281-- **Conciseness:** Keep docs actionable, not verbose
282-

file.content:
### State Management: AVOID React Hooks, USE @mms/toolkit

> **CRITICAL RULE:** Avoid `useState`/`useRef` for state. Use `@mms/toolkit` state-machine for ALL state.

**Why?**
- React hooks cause "stale closures" in animation loops
- State updates are batched/async - values become stale
- Debugging is hard when state is scattered across multiple hooks
- @mms/toolkit uses `useSyncExternalStore` - always synchronous and consistent

**Current architecture (after TECH-DEBT-001):**
```
apps/canvas/src/state/
├── canvas-machine.ts   # Main machine
├── viewport.ts         # Slice: camera, zoom, momentum, animation
├── interaction.ts      # Slice: hover, selection, mouse position
└── index.ts            # Re-exports
```

**How to use:**
```typescript
// ✅ CORRECT: Use state-machine
import { useCanvasSelector, canvasStore, canvasActions } from '../state';

// Read state (reactive - causes re-render on change)
const zoom = useCanvasSelector(s => s.viewport.camera.zoom);

// Read state (synchronous - for animation loops)
const camera = canvasStore.getState().viewport.camera;

// Update state
canvasStore.dispatch(canvasActions.viewport.setCamera({ x, y, zoom }));
```

```typescript
// ❌ AVOID: React hooks for state
const [zoom, setZoom] = useState(1); // NO - stale closures
const zoomRef = useRef(1);           // NO - unnecessary workaround
```

**Exceptions (use hooks only for):**
- `useCallback`/`useMemo` for render optimization
- `useEffect` for side effects (event listeners, cleanup)
- `useRef` ONLY for DOM references (`containerRef`)
- 100% local disposable UI state (e.g., temporary tooltip position)

## Quick Commands

> ⚠️ **ALWAYS run `./init.sh --no-server` first** to verify environment.

### Production App (apps/canvas)

```bash
# 1. Initialize and verify environment
./init.sh --no-server

# 2. Install dependencies
bun i

# 3. Run production app (http://localhost:5173)
cd apps/canvas && bun run dev

# 4. Build for production
cd apps/canvas && bun run build

# 5. Type check
bun run typecheck
```

### POC Reference

```bash
# View the POC (reference only)
open design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html
```

### Troubleshooting

This is a **monorepo**. If root scripts fail, run directly in the app:

input.new_string:
**Exceptions (use hooks only for):**
- `useCallback`/`useMemo` for render optimization
- `useEffect` for side effects (event listeners, cleanup)
- `useRef` ONLY for DOM references (`containerRef`)
- 100% local disposable UI state (e.g., temporary tooltip position)

### High-Performance Architecture (STATE-ARCH-001)

> **Research:** See `docs/state-management-research/relatorio-state-management.pdf`

The research document identifies **Garbage Collection as the primary bottleneck** in WebGL applications, not rendering. Traditional React patterns (useState, Proxy-based stores) are inadequate for 60fps updates on mobile.

**Planned Dual-Store Architecture:**

| Aspect | Simulation State | Application State |
|--------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Purpose | Entity data (60fps) | UI state (event-driven) |
| Technology | TypedArrays / SoA pattern | **@mms/toolkit** (NEVER Zustand) |
| Mutability | Direct (performance) | Immutable (Immer) |
| Access | Indices / getState() | useSelector / subscriptions |

**Key Principles from Research:**

1. **Structure of Arrays (SoA):** Store entity properties in separate TypedArrays for cache-friendly iteration
2. **Object Pooling:** Pre-allocate vectors/objects to eliminate GC pauses in animation loops
3. **Spatial Indexing:** Use RBush (R-tree) for O(log n) viewport queries
4. **UI Throttling:** Sync Engine→UI at 30fps max, not every frame

**CRITICAL: Library Choice**

```
✅ UI State:     @mms/toolkit (packages/toolkit)
✅ Entity Data:  Custom TypedArray store (to be implemented)
✅ Undo/Redo:    Command Pattern + Immer patches

❌ NEVER USE:    Zustand, Redux, MobX, Valtio
                 (despite PDF recommendations, we use @mms/toolkit)
```

**Implementation:** See `features.json` → `STATE-ARCH-001` for phased plan.

## Quick Commands

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Metro Integrated Planning System (MMS)

## Project Overview
High-Performance Enterprise Integrated Planning System with "Semantic Zoom" UX (Google Earth-style) for metro fleet management. Renders 50+ trains and 10 years of tasks at constant 60fps using WebGL.

---

## HARNESS: Fundamental Concept (READ THIS FIRST)

> **This project uses a Harness.** If you don't know what that is, STOP and research:
> - [Effective harnesses for long-running agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-harnesses-for-long-running-agents)

### What is a Harness?

A **Harness** is a framework that enables AI agents to work on long tasks across multiple context windows/sessions.

**Problem:** Each new Claude Code session starts with NO memory of previous sessions.

**Solution:** State files persist context between sessions:
- `claude-progress.txt` → session history
- `features.json` → requirements with status (passes: true/false)

### Work Protocol

```
1. READ claude-progress.txt (understand current state)
2. READ features.json (find next feature)
3. IMPLEMENT one feature
4. TEST visually (Playwright MCP)
5. COMMIT after passing
6. UPDATE claude-progress.txt
7. STOP (leave codebase production-ready)
```

### Self-Correction Protocol

If you don't understand what harness is after reading this:
1. **SEARCH:** WebSearch "Anthropic harness agents 2025"
2. **ANALYZE:** Why was local documentation unclear?
3. **FIX:** Update CLAUDE.md or hooks to improve clarity
4. **DOCUMENT:** Record the fix in claude-progress.txt

### Harness Files

| File                  | Purpose              | When to Read       |
|-----------------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| `claude-progress.txt` | Session history      | **ALWAYS first**   |
| `features.json`       | Features with status | After progress.txt |
| `CLAUDE.md`           | Project rules        | Reference          |

---

## Parallel Execution (Git Worktrees)

This project supports **parallel feature development** using git worktrees. This enables multiple Claude sessions to work on different features simultaneously, achieving ~2.5x faster development.

### When to Use Parallel Execution

Use parallel execution when:
- Multiple independent features can be worked on concurrently
- Features modify different files (low conflict risk)
- You want to maximize development throughput

### Quick Start

```bash
# Show current status of all waves/features
bun scripts/parallel-runner.ts status

# Execute Wave 1 (starts 3 parallel groups)
bun scripts/parallel-runner.ts wave 1

# Merge a completed feature back to main
bun scripts/parallel-runner.ts merge F023

# Clean up all worktrees
bun scripts/parallel-runner.ts cleanup
```

### Wave Structure

Features are organized into 4 waves based on dependencies and file conflicts:

```
WAVE 1 (No dependencies - 3 parallel groups):
├── Group A: F023 (Dependency lines) → MicroLayer.tsx
├── Group B: F123, F025 (Minimap) → App.tsx [sequential]
└── Group C: F016, F017 (Scroll modifiers) → InfiniteCanvas.tsx [sequential]

WAVE 2 (After Wave 1 - 2 parallel groups):
├── Group D: F027, F028 (Keyboard shortcuts) → InfiniteCanvas.tsx [sequential]
└── Group E: F026 (Task details panel) → App.tsx

WAVE 3 (After Wave 2 - 1 group):
└── Group F: F021 (Keyboard navigation)

WAVE 4 (After Wave 3 - 1 group):
└── Group G: F022 (ARIA labels)
```

### How It Works

1. **Worktrees**: Each feature gets its own directory (`.trees/<feature-id>/`) with a dedicated branch
2. **Isolation**: Each Claude session only modifies files for its assigned feature
3. **Merging**: After a feature is complete, it's merged back to `main`
4. **Dependencies**: Waves are executed in order; a wave can't start until the previous completes

### Key Files

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `scripts/parallel-runner.ts` | Interactive runner with worktree management |
| `scripts/parallel-config.ts` | Wave/group definitions and conflict analysis |
| `docs/PARALLEL_EXECUTION.md` | Complete documentation |
| `.trees/` | Directory for git worktrees (gitignored) |

### Conflict Risk Levels

- **LOW**: Features modify isolated files (e.g., only MicroLayer.tsx)
- **MEDIUM**: Features modify shared files but different sections (e.g., App.tsx layout)
- **HIGH**: Features modify the same functions (e.g., handleWheel in InfiniteCanvas.tsx)

Sequential groups (marked `[sequential]`) contain features that must run one after another due to HIGH conflict risk.

**Full documentation:** See `docs/PARALLEL_EXECUTION.md`

---

## Security & Permissions

### File Access Restrictions

This project uses **deny permissions** to prevent access to personal directories outside the project.

**Protected directories** (access denied):
- `~/Desktop`, `~/Documents`, `~/Downloads`
- `~/Pictures`, `~/Movies`, `~/Music`
- Any `find ~` or `find /Users` commands

**Configuration:** `.claude/settings.local.json` contains deny rules that block:
- Read/Write/Edit/Glob/Grep operations on protected paths
- Bash commands that scan home directories

### Playwright MCP Configuration

Visual testing uses Playwright MCP in **headless mode** to avoid stealing focus.

**Configuration:** `.mcp.json` (project root)
```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "playwright": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["@playwright/mcp@latest", "--headless"]
    }
  }
}
```

**Benefits:**
- Browser runs invisibly in background
- Does not interrupt user's work
- Screenshots captured without window popup

---

## KISS Philosophy

**KISS is NOT "do the minimum". It IS "don't add complexity that blocks progress".**

The goal is **speed of delivery with quality**. The product must impress the client to win contracts.

### What KISS means (examples)

| ✅ DO                                                         | ❌ DON'T                                                          |
|--------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Add polish, debug tools, UX refinements that accelerate work | Spend days on infrastructure before having something presentable |
| Use best practices that prevent future problems              | Add abstractions for hypothetical future needs                   |
| Preserve knowledge/tips for when they become relevant        | Ignore proven patterns just because "not needed yet"             |

### When to Increase Complexity (examples)

| ✅ Increase complexity when...     | ❌ Do NOT increase when... |
|-----------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Current approach blocks a feature | "It would be cleaner"     |
| Performance is unacceptable       | "Best practices say so"   |
| Code duplication becomes painful  | Premature optimization    |
| Team needs shared abstractions    | Speculative future needs  |

### Example: POC-first approach

When the monorepo setup consumed days debugging config, a pure HTML POC (`design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html`) was created to validate the concept quickly.

**Lesson:** Start simple, validate fast, then add complexity incrementally.

---

## Production App: apps/canvas

The production application is located at `apps/canvas/` - a React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS 6 app that implements the "Semantic Zoom" UX concept.

### Product Entry Point

**`apps/canvas`** - The production React app with semantic zoom.

```bash
# Run development server (http://localhost:5173)
cd apps/canvas && bun run dev

# Or from root
bun run dev --filter=@mms/canvas
```

### Tech Stack

| Technology    | Version | Purpose                                   |
|---------------|---------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Vite**      | 5.x     | Build tool and dev server                 |
| **React**     | 18.3.1  | UI framework (required for BlueprintJS 6) |
| **BlueprintJS** | 6.1.0 | UI component library                      |
| **R3F**       | 8.x     | React Three Fiber for WebGL               |
| **drei**      | 9.x     | R3F helpers and components                |
| **Three.js**  | 0.170.x | 3D rendering engine                       |

### Features

| Feature       | Description                                         | LOD Level |
|---------------|-----------------------------------------------------|-----------|
| **Pan**       | Drag to navigate through time (X) and trains (Y)    | All       |
| **Zoom**      | Scroll to zoom in/out, zoom centers on cursor       | All       |
| **Macro View** | Heatmap showing task density (yellow→orange→red)   | < 0.5x    |
| **Meso View** | DNA-like task strips with colored blocks            | 0.5x - 2x |
| **Micro View** | Detailed task cards with labels and grid           | > 2x      |
| **Sidebar**   | Train IDs synced with vertical pan (BlueprintJS Card) | All     |
| **Timeline**  | Date labels with adaptive granularity               | All       |

### Roadmap

See `features.json` for complete feature list. Key phases:

```
✅ F001-F015:  POC validation (archived)
🔄 F101-F103:  Foundation - Vite + React 18 + BlueprintJS + R3F ← CURRENT
⏳ F104-F107:  Core rendering - Data, MesoLayer, pan/zoom, LOD
⏳ F108-F110:  All LOD layers - Macro, Micro, transitions
⏳ F111-F114:  UI integration - Sidebar, timeline, controls
⏳ F115-F117:  Polish - Instructions, performance, visual parity
```

---

## POC Reference

A standalone HTML demo exists for visual reference only:

**`design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html`** - Validated prototype.

```bash
# View the POC reference
open design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html    # macOS
```

Use as visual reference for:
- Appearance of semantic zoom UX
- 3 LOD levels (Macro/Meso/Micro)
- Layout and visual design

---

### Key Rule

**Do NOT write throwaway code without clear time/agility gains.** If code will be rewritten anyway, the simple version must provide faster iteration or validation—otherwise, just build it right the first time.
**If you know something will cause problems later (performance, UX, maintainability), address it now based on knowledge and testing—not fear, not speculation.**

---

## Documentation Rules

- **Language:** Always write documentation in English
- **Location:** Prefer updating existing docs over creating new files
- **Conciseness:** Keep docs actionable, not verbose

---

## Tech Stack

### Production App (apps/canvas)
- **Build Tool:** Vite 5.x
- **Frontend:** React 18.3.1 (required for BlueprintJS 6)
- **UI Components:** BlueprintJS 6.1.0 (Palantir)
- **Graphics Engine:** @react-three/fiber 8.x, @react-three/drei 9.x, three 0.170.x
- **State Management:** Custom state-machine (packages/toolkit)

### Monorepo Infrastructure
- **Runtime:** Bun 1.2.0
- **Monorepo:** Turborepo
- **Shared Package:** @mms/toolkit (React 18 || 19 compatible)

### State Management: AVOID React Hooks, USE @mms/toolkit

> **CRITICAL RULE:** Avoid `useState`/`useRef` for state. Use `@mms/toolkit` state-machine for ALL state.

**Why?**
- React hooks cause "stale closures" in animation loops
- State updates are batched/async - values become stale
- Debugging is hard when state is scattered across multiple hooks
- @mms/toolkit uses `useSyncExternalStore` - always synchronous and consistent

**Current architecture (after TECH-DEBT-001):**
```
apps/canvas/src/state/
├── canvas-machine.ts   # Main machine
├── viewport.ts         # Slice: camera, zoom, momentum, animation
├── interaction.ts      # Slice: hover, selection, mouse position
└── index.ts            # Re-exports
```

**How to use:**
```typescript
// ✅ CORRECT: Use state-machine
import { useCanvasSelector, canvasStore, canvasActions } from '../state';

// Read state (reactive - causes re-render on change)
const zoom = useCanvasSelector(s => s.viewport.camera.zoom);

// Read state (synchronous - for animation loops)
const camera = canvasStore.getState().viewport.camera;

// Update state
canvasStore.dispatch(canvasActions.viewport.setCamera({ x, y, zoom }));
```

```typescript
// ❌ AVOID: React hooks for state
const [zoom, setZoom] = useState(1); // NO - stale closures
const zoomRef = useRef(1);           // NO - unnecessary workaround
```

**Exceptions (use hooks only for):**
- `useCallback`/`useMemo` for render optimization
- `useEffect` for side effects (event listeners, cleanup)
- `useRef` ONLY for DOM references (`containerRef`)
- 100% local disposable UI state (e.g., temporary tooltip position)

## Quick Commands

> ⚠️ **ALWAYS run `./init.sh --no-server` first** to verify environment.

### Production App (apps/canvas)

```bash
# 1. Initialize and verify environment
./init.sh --no-server

# 2. Install dependencies
bun i

# 3. Run production app (http://localhost:5173)
cd apps/canvas && bun run dev

# 4. Build for production
cd apps/canvas && bun run build

# 5. Type check
bun run typecheck
```

### POC Reference

```bash
# View the POC (reference only)
open design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html
```

### Troubleshooting

This is a **monorepo**. If root scripts fail, run directly in the app:

```bash
# Direct command (always works)
cd apps/canvas && bun run dev

# Check what scripts exist
cat apps/canvas/package.json | grep -A10 '"scripts"'
```

---

## CRITICAL AI RULES

> **READ THIS FIRST.** These rules address fundamental AI limitations.

### Rule 1: CHECK THE CURRENT DATE

**Your training data is outdated.** Before starting ANY work:

```bash
date  # Check actual current date
```

**DO NOT assume the current year is your training year.** If your training was in 2024 but it's now 2025:
- Documentation has changed
- Library versions have changed
- APIs have changed
- Best practices have evolved

**Always include the CURRENT YEAR in documentation searches.**

### Rule 2: YOU CANNOT SEE THE UI

**You are blind to rendered output.** You MUST use MCP visual testing.

```
What you know:              Reality:
✓ Code compiled             ✗ Blank white screen
✓ No TypeScript errors      ✗ React error boundary
✓ Tests passed              ✗ Component doesn't render
```

**NEVER mark a UI feature as complete without MCP screenshot verification.**

### Rule 3: INSTALL DEPENDENCIES VIA COMMANDS

**NEVER manually edit dependency files to add packages.**

```bash
# CORRECT
bun add react
bun add @react-three/fiber

# WRONG - DO NOT DO THIS
# Manually editing package.json: "react": "^18.0.0"
```

Why: Package managers resolve correct versions and peer dependencies.

### Rule 4: RESEARCH BEFORE GUESSING

**When stuck, STOP and research.** Don't try random solutions.

See the STOP-SEARCH-UNDERSTAND-APPLY method below.

### Rule 5: SEARCH FOR UPDATED DOCUMENTATION

**Your training data is stale.** For complex configurations:

```bash
# Always search with current year
WebSearch: "[technology] [topic] documentation 2025"
WebFetch: [official docs URL]
```

**Do NOT rely on training data for:**
- Library configurations
- API patterns
- Framework setup
- Plugin systems

### Rule 6: VERIFY COMMANDS BEFORE RUNNING

**NEVER assume a command will work.** Before running ANY command:

```bash
# WRONG - Running blindly
bun run dev  # ← May fail if monorepo filter is wrong

# CORRECT - Verify first
cat package.json | grep -A10 '"scripts"'  # Check what scripts exist
./init.sh --no-server                      # Verify environment
bun run dev                                 # Then run
```

**If a command fails:**
1. **READ the error message** - Don't retry blindly
2. **Check the configuration** - Is the command correct for this project?
3. **Try the direct approach** - `cd apps/canvas && bun run dev`
4. **Document the solution** - Update relevant docs so future sessions don't repeat the error

**Common monorepo gotchas:**
- Root `package.json` scripts may use filters that don't match package names
- Always check `apps/*/package.json` for the actual package name
- When in doubt, run commands directly in the package folder

---

## MCP VISUAL TESTING (MANDATORY)

### Why This is Required

You cannot see the app you're building. Without visual testing, you will:
- Mark blank screens as "working"
- Miss CSS/layout issues
- Overlook runtime errors visible only in UI

### Testing Protocol: Playwright MCP

Use **Playwright MCP** for visual tests. Runs headless and **does not steal focus** from the user's window.

#### Step 1: Navigate to the page

```
mcp__playwright__browser_navigate url="file:///full/path/to/file.html"
```

Or for local server:
```
mcp__playwright__browser_navigate url="http://localhost:5173"
```

#### Step 2: Check console (JS errors)

```
mcp__playwright__browser_console_messages level="error"
```

- Red errors → **FAIL** (investigate before continuing)
- Yellow warnings → Evaluate (e.g., "Tailwind CDN production warning" is OK)

#### Step 3: Capture screenshot

```
mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot filename="screenshots/test-name.png"
```

For scrollable pages:
```
mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot fullPage=true filename="screenshots/test-full.png"
```

#### Step 4: Analyze visually

Check in the screenshot:
- [ ] Content visible? (not blank/empty)
- [ ] Layout correct? (elements positioned as expected)
- [ ] Colors correct? (matching design)
- [ ] No visual artifacts?

#### Step 5: Interactions (if needed)

```
mcp__playwright__browser_click element="button X" ref="e123"
mcp__playwright__browser_type element="field Y" ref="e456" text="value"
```

#### Step 6: Close browser at the end

```
mcp__playwright__browser_close
```

### Approval Criteria

| Criterion | PASS | FAIL |
|-----------|------|------|
| Console without errors | ✅ | ❌ Red errors |
| Content rendered | ✅ | ❌ Blank/empty screen |
| Layout correct | ✅ | ❌ Misaligned elements |
| JS executed | ✅ | ❌ Dynamic content missing |

### Final Decision

```
Visual OK + Console clean    → passes: true
Visual OK + Console warnings → Evaluate (may pass)
Visual OK + Console errors   → FAIL (fix it)
Visual broken                → FAIL (fix it)
```

### Playwright MCP Tools

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `mcp__playwright__browser_navigate` | Open page (file:// or http://) |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot` | Capture viewport or fullPage |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_snapshot` | Accessibility tree (debug) |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_console_messages` | View JS errors/warnings |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_click` | Click on element |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_type` | Type in field |
| `mcp__playwright__browser_close` | Close browser |


---

## ERROR HANDLING: STOP-SEARCH-UNDERSTAND-APPLY

### Classify the Error First

| Error Type | Action |
|------------|--------|
| Typo, syntax, obvious | Fix directly |
| Config, runtime, library, unknown | **RESEARCH FIRST** |

### The Method

```
1. STOP
   - Recognize you're stuck
   - Do NOT try random solutions

2. SEARCH
   - WebSearch: "[error] + [tech] + [CURRENT YEAR]"
   - WebFetch: Official documentation
   - Include current year, not training year

3. UNDERSTAND
   - Read results carefully
   - Identify ROOT CAUSE
   - Know WHY before fixing

4. APPLY
   - Implement correct solution
   - From documentation, not guessing

5. DOCUMENT
   - Update relevant docs if this is a recurring issue
```

---

## Project Structure

```
system/
├── apps/
│   └── canvas/           # Production app (Vite + React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS)
│       ├── src/
│       │   ├── main.tsx          # Entry point
│       │   ├── App.tsx           # Main component
│       │   ├── components/       # BlueprintJS UI components
│       │   ├── canvas/           # R3F rendering
│       │   │   ├── InfiniteCanvas.tsx
│       │   │   ├── CameraController.tsx
│       │   │   └── layers/       # Macro, Meso, Micro
│       │   ├── data/             # Types and generator
│       │   └── hooks/            # useViewport, etc.
│       ├── index.html
│       ├── vite.config.ts
│       └── package.json
├── packages/
│   └── toolkit/          # State machine, utilities (React 18 || 19)
├── design/
│   └── reference/        # POC and visual references
│       └── semantic-zoom-poc.html

├── claude-progress.txt   # Session history

```

## Key Files

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `apps/canvas/src/main.tsx` | Production app entry |
| `apps/canvas/src/App.tsx` | Main React component |
| `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx` | R3F canvas |
| `apps/canvas/src/canvas/layers/*.tsx` | LOD rendering layers |
| `apps/canvas/src/data/generator.ts` | Mock data generation |
| `packages/toolkit/` | Shared state machine |
| `design/reference/semantic-zoom-poc.html` | POC (visual reference only) |

## LOD System (Level of Detail)

The system implements three rendering layers based on camera zoom:

- **Macro (zoom < 0.5):** Procedural shader heatmap - 1 draw call
- **Meso (0.5 ≤ zoom ≤ 2.0):** InstancedMesh with 60k instances
- **Micro (zoom > 2.0):** HTML overlay with Line2 dependency curves

## Current State

Read `claude-progress.txt` for session history and `features.json` for requirements status.

- ./docs/RESEARCH.md - Industry research and best practices for systems like this.

### Technical Documentation (`agent_docs/`)
- @agent_docs/architecture.md - Architecture details
- @agent_docs/testing.md - Testing protocol
- @agent_docs/conventions.md - Code conventions
- @agent_docs/ai-limitations.md - AI limitations and mitigations

lines: - 100% local disposable UI state (e.g., temporary tooltip position)

+The research document identifies **Garbage Collection as the primary bottleneck** in WebGL applications, not rendering. Traditional React patterns (useState, Proxy-based stores) are inadequate for 60fps updates on mobile.
+1. **Structure of Arrays (SoA):** Store entity properties in separate TypedArrays for cache-friendly iteration
+2. **Object Pooling:** Pre-allocate vectors/objects to eliminate GC pauses in animation loops
+3. **Spatial Indexing:** Use RBush (R-tree) for O(log n) viewport queries
+4. **UI Throttling:** Sync Engine→UI at 30fps max, not every frame
+✅ Entity Data:  Custom TypedArray store (to be implemented)
+❌ NEVER USE:    Zustand, Redux, MobX, Valtio

# Metro Integrated Planning System - Progress Log

## Session: 2025-12-17 (UX Bug Fixes) - IN PROGRESS 🔄

### UX Bug Fix Sprint

**Context:**
User reported multiple UX bugs after features were marked complete. This session addresses all reported issues.

**Bugs Tracked in features.json:**
- BUG-001: Zoom freezes/stops working after interactions (PENDING)
- BUG-002: Sidebar (train list) doesn't have scroll (PENDING)
- BUG-003: Trackpad 2-finger scroll only zooms, should pan (PENDING)
- BUG-004: Minimap not like Google Maps style (PENDING)
- BUG-005: Date values not visible at certain zoom levels (PENDING)
- BUG-006: PerfMonitor blocks UI (FIXED ✅)
- BUG-007: Sidebar/train row alignment (PENDING)
- BUG-008: Elastic/momentum drag bug (FIXED ✅)

**Fixes Applied:**

1. **BUG-008 FIXED** - Disabled momentum panning in `InfiniteCanvas.tsx`:
   - Commented out `startMomentum()` call in `handlePointerUp`
   - Momentum was causing elastic/jumping behavior despite cameraPosRef fix
   - Can be reimplemented later with proper state synchronization

2. **BUG-006 FIXED** - Moved PerfMonitor from `top-right` to `bottom-left`:
   - Changed default position in `PerfMonitor.tsx`
   - Prevents blocking navbar controls (Reset View, Perf button)

**Harness State Saved:**
- All bugs added to features.json with priority 0
- BUG-006, BUG-008 marked as passes: true
- Remaining bugs have passes: false

**Pending Fixes (Priority Order):**
1. BUG-003: Add 2-finger pan support (without modifiers)
2. BUG-002: Fix sidebar scroll (overflow-y)
3. BUG-007: Fix sidebar/train row alignment
4. BUG-004: Refactor minimap to Google Maps style (no X, always visible)
5. BUG-005: Fix date visibility at extreme zoom
6. BUG-001: Test zoom after other fixes

**Files Changed:**
- `apps/canvas/src/canvas/InfiniteCanvas.tsx` (disabled momentum)
- `apps/canvas/src/components/PerfMonitor.tsx` (moved to bottom-left)
- `features.json` (added BUG-001 through BUG-008)

**Dev Server:** http://localhost:5177

# Metro Integrated Planning System - Progress Log

## Session: 2025-12-17 (STATE-ARCH-001 Planning) - COMPLETE ✅

### STATE-ARCH-001: High-Performance State Management Architecture - PLANNED

**Objective:**
Plan refactoring of state management architecture based on research document analyzing Figma, Google Maps, CesiumJS, and WebGL game engines.

**Research Document:**
- `docs/state-management-research/relatorio-state-management.pdf`
- Key finding: GC (Garbage Collection) is the primary bottleneck, not rendering
- React Hooks and reactive stores inadequate for 60fps on mobile
- Recommends Dual-Store: Simulation State (TypedArrays) + Application State (external store)

**Task Created: STATE-ARCH-001**

Added comprehensive task to features.json with 5 implementation phases:

1. **Phase 1 - Foundation:** Create EntityStore with TypedArrays (Structure of Arrays pattern)
2. **Phase 2 - Spatial Index:** Implement RBush R-tree for viewport queries
3. **Phase 3 - UI Bridge:** Connect Engine State to @mms/toolkit (NOT Zustand as PDF suggests)
4. **Phase 4 - Object Pooling:** Eliminate GC pauses in animation loops
5. **Phase 5 - Undo/Redo:** Command Pattern + Immer patches

**CRITICAL DECISION: @mms/toolkit over Zustand**

The PDF recommends Zustand for UI state, but this project uses **@mms/toolkit** (packages/toolkit):
- Already implemented and integrated
- Uses Immer for immutability
- Uses useSyncExternalStore for synchronous updates
- Has slice-based architecture matching project needs

**Files Changed:**
- `features.json` - Added STATE-ARCH-001 task with detailed implementation plan
- `CLAUDE.md` - Added "High-Performance Architecture" section with guidelines

**Performance Targets:**
- 100,000+ entities renderable
- 60fps constant during pan/zoom
- <100MB memory for 100k entities
- <5ms viewport queries
- Zero perceptible GC pauses

**Next Steps:**
- Implementation will be phased over multiple sessions
- Start with Phase 1 (EntityStore) when prioritized

---

## Session: 2025-12-17 (UX Bug Fixes) - IN PROGRESS 🔄

5. **Phase 5 - Undo/Redo:** Command Pattern + Immer patches

**CRITICAL DECISION: @mms/toolkit over Zustand**

The PDF recommends Zustand for UI state, but this project uses **@mms/toolkit** (packages/toolkit):
- Already implemented and integrated
- Uses Immer for immutability
- Uses useSyncExternalStore for synchronous updates
- Has slice-based architecture matching project needs

**Files Changed:**
- `features.json` - Added STATE-ARCH-001 task with detailed implementation plan
- `CLAUDE.md` - Added "High-Performance Architecture" section with guidelines

**Performance Targets:**
- 100,000+ entities renderable
- 60fps constant during pan/zoom
- <100MB memory for 100k entities
- <5ms viewport queries
- Zero perceptible GC pauses

**Next Steps:**
- Implementation will be phased over multiple sessions
- Start with Phase 1 (EntityStore) when prioritized

## Session: 2025-12-17 (Parallel Harness Setup) - COMPLETE ✅

### HARNESS-005: Parallel Execution Infrastructure - COMPLETE

**Objective:**
Create infrastructure to run multiple features in parallel using git worktrees, enabling ~2.5x faster development.

**Research:**
- Studied [Anthropic's Effective Harnesses for Long-Running Agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-harnesses-for-long-running-agents)
- Researched git worktree patterns for AI agent parallel development
- Analyzed file conflict potential for all 10 pending features

**Analysis of Pending Features (10 total):**

```
WAVE 1 (Can run in parallel - 3 groups):
├── Group A: F023 (Dependency lines) → MicroLayer.tsx [LOW risk]
├── Group B: F123, F025 (Minimap) → App.tsx [MEDIUM risk, sequential]
└── Group C: F016, F017 (Scroll modifiers) → InfiniteCanvas.tsx [HIGH risk, sequential]

WAVE 2 (After Wave 1 merge - 2 groups):
├── Group D: F027, F028 (Keyboard shortcuts) → InfiniteCanvas.tsx [HIGH risk, sequential]
└── Group E: F026 (Task details panel) → App.tsx [MEDIUM risk]

WAVE 3 (After Wave 2 merge - 1 group):
└── Group F: F021 (Keyboard task navigation) → Multiple files [HIGH risk]

WAVE 4 (After Wave 3 merge - 1 group):
└── Group G: F022 (ARIA labels) → Multiple files [LOW risk]
```

**Files Created:**
- `scripts/parallel-config.ts` - Wave/group configuration with conflict analysis
- `scripts/parallel-runner.ts` - Interactive parallel execution runner
- `docs/PARALLEL_EXECUTION.md` - Complete documentation

**Files Modified:**
- `.gitignore` - Added `.trees/` for worktree directories
- `features.json` - Added `parallelExecution` section with waves configuration

**Key Features of Parallel Runner:**
1. **Git worktree management** - Creates isolated working directories
2. **Terminal session spawning** - Opens separate Claude sessions per feature
3. **Isolation prompts** - Each session restricted to its feature scope
4. **Wave dependencies** - Prevents starting Wave N before Wave N-1 completes
5. **Merge helper** - Automates feature branch merging
6. **Status display** - Shows progress across all waves/groups
7. **macOS notifications** - Alerts for session events
8. **Cleanup command** - Removes all worktrees

**Speedup Calculation:**
- Sequential: 10 features = 10 sessions
- Parallel: 4 waves (2+2+1+1) = ~4 "effective sessions"
- Estimated speedup: **2.5x**

**Usage:**
```bash
# Show status
bun scripts/parallel-runner.ts status

# Execute Wave 1
bun scripts/parallel-runner.ts wave 1

# Merge completed feature
bun scripts/parallel-runner.ts merge F023
```
